examples.title: Stakeholder Analysis Process
examples.description: Algorithm for conducting systematic stakeholder analysis during groundwork phase
examples.code:
PROCEDURE ConductStakeholderAnalysis()
 stakeholderList ← EMPTY_LIST
 
 // Step 1: Identify all potential stakeholders
 potentialStakeholders ← IdentifyFromProjectCharter()
 potentialStakeholders ← potentialStakeholders ∪ IdentifyFromDomainExperts()
 potentialStakeholders ← potentialStakeholders ∪ IdentifyFromSimilarProjects()
 
 // Step 2: Gather information for each stakeholder
 FOR EACH stakeholder IN potentialStakeholders
 info ← GatherStakeholderInfo(stakeholder)
 // Collect: name, role, organization, power, interest, attitude, needs
 stakeholderRegister.Add(info)
 END FOR
 
 // Step 3: Classify stakeholders using Power/Interest Grid
 FOR EACH stakeholder IN stakeholderRegister
 classification ← ClassifyStakeholder(stakeholder.power, stakeholder.interest)
 // Returns: ManageClosely, KeepSatisfied, KeepInformed, Monitor
 stakeholder.classification ← classification
 strategy ← GetEngagementStrategy(classification)
 stakeholder.communicationPlan ← strategy
 END FOR
 
 // Step 4: Document and communicate plan
 GenerateStakeholderManagementPlan(stakeholderRegister)
 RETURN stakeholderRegister
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: This pseudocode outlines the systematic process for stakeholder analysis: first identifying all potential stakeholders from multiple sources, then gathering detailed information to populate the stakeholder register, classifying each stakeholder using the power/interest grid to determine appropriate engagement strategies, and finally documenting the stakeholder management plan.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) where n is the number of potential stakeholders

examples.title: Requirements Change Control Process
examples.description: Formal process for handling changes to baselined requirements
examples.code:
PROCEDURE HandleRequirementsChange(changeRequest)
 // Step 1: Validate change request submission
 IF NOT changeRequest.IsComplete() THEN
 RETURN Reject("Incomplete change request form")
 END IF
 
 // Step 2: Perform impact analysis
 affectedRequirements ← TraceToParentRequirements(changeRequest.requirementID)
 affectedDesign ← TraceToDesignElements(affectedRequirements)
 affectedCode ← TraceToCodeModules(affectedDesign)
 affectedTests ← TraceToTestCases(affectedRequirements)
 
 impactReport ← GenerateImpactReport(
 affectedRequirements.size,
 affectedDesign.size,
 affectedCode.size,
 affectedTests.size
 )
 
 // Step 3: Submit to Change Control Board
 changeRequest.impactAnalysis ← impactReport
 decision ← ChangeControlBoard.Evaluate(changeRequest)
 
 // Step 4: Process decision
 IF decision == APPROVED THEN
 UpdateRequirementsBaseline(changeRequest)
 UpdateTraceabilityMatrix()
 NotifyStakeholders(changeRequest)
 RETURN Approve(changeRequest)
 ELSE IF decision == REJECTED THEN
 RETURN Reject(decision.reason)
 ELSE
 // Requires revision
 RETURN RequestRevision(decision.feedback)
 END IF
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: This pseudocode demonstrates the formal change control process: validating the change request, performing comprehensive impact analysis using the traceability matrix to identify all affected artifacts, presenting to the Change Control Board for decision, and upon approval, updating the baseline and notifying stakeholders.
examples.timeComplexity: O(t) where t is the number of traced artifacts in the traceability matrix
examples.spaceComplexity: O(t) for storing impact analysis results

examples.title: Requirements Prioritization Using MoSCoW
examples.description: Algorithm for prioritizing requirements using the MoSCoW technique
examples.code:
PROCEDURE PrioritizeRequirements(requirementsList, stakeholders)
 // Step 1: Gather priority input from stakeholders
 priorityMatrix ← EMPTY_MAP
 
 FOR EACH requirement IN requirementsList
 votes ← EMPTY_LIST
 FOR EACH stakeholder IN stakeholders
 IF stakeholder.CanVote(requirement) THEN
 votes.Add(stakeholder.Vote(requirement))
 END IF
 END FOR
 priorityMatrix[requirement] ← AggregateVotes(votes)
 END FOR
 
 // Step 2: Apply MoSCoW categorization
 sortedRequirements ← SortByPriority(priorityMatrix, DESCENDING)
 totalRequirements ← sortedRequirements.length
 
 mustHave ← sortedRequirements[0 : floor(totalRequirements * 0.60)]
 shouldHave ← sortedRequirements[floor(totalRequirements * 0.60) : floor(totalRequirements * 0.80)]
 couldHave ← sortedRequirements[floor(totalRequirements * 0.80) : floor(totalRequirements * 0.95)]
 wontHave ← sortedRequirements[floor(totalRequirements * 0.95) : totalRequirements]
 
 // Step 3: Validate against constraints
 estimatedCost ← EstimateCost(mustHave)
 IF estimatedCost > budget THEN
 AdjustCategories(mustHave, shouldHave)
 END IF
 
 // Step 4: Document and obtain agreement
 prioritizationReport ← CreateReport(mustHave, shouldHave, couldHave, wontHave)
 StakeholderSignOff(prioritizationReport)
 
 RETURN prioritizationReport
END PROCEDURE
examples.explanation: This pseudocode illustrates requirements prioritization: gathering priority votes from stakeholders, sorting requirements, applying MoSCoW categorization based on priority, validating against budget constraints, and obtaining stakeholder sign-off. The 60/20/15/5 split is a common heuristic that should be adjusted based on project context.
examples.timeComplexity: O(r × s) where r is requirements count and s is stakeholder count
